schools.summary: Paul's lofty exhortation (drawing the consequence 'therefore' from 4:32): 'be ye followers (mimetai — imitators) of God' — believers are to imitate God himself, especially in his forgiving love and goodness; 'as dear (beloved) children' — as children naturally imitate a loved father, so believers, God's beloved children, are to copy their Father, the relationship making the imitation fitting (Barnes, Robertson, Henry).
Calvin: nothing less than the imitation of God himself is set before the believer; the dignity of being his children calls for likeness to him in love and goodness.

themes: Be ye therefore followers of God, as dear children
the call to imitate God
as beloved children copying their Father
the imitation of God

schools.summary: The command: 'walk (order your whole life) in love, as (after the pattern of) Christ also hath loved us' — Christ's love is the model and measure of the believer's love; his love shown in that he 'gave himself for us an offering and a sacrifice to God' — his self-surrender on the cross, a true sacrifice (the terms drawn from the Levitical offerings) presented to God on our behalf; 'for a sweetsmelling savour' — a fragrant offering, well-pleasing and acceptable to God (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: Christ's sacrifice was the sweet savour that reconciled God to us; the believer's love is to be patterned on this self-giving, ordering the whole walk by the love that gave itself for us.

themes: walk in love, as Christ also hath loved us
hath given himself for us an offering and a sacrifice to God for a sweetsmelling savour
walking in love after Christ's self-sacrifice
Christ's love the pattern

schools.summary: Paul, turning from love to its opposite, forbids 'fornication, and all uncleanness (akatharsia — impurity of every kind), or covetousness (pleonexia — greed, often linked with impurity)' — the sins of lust and grasping desire; these are not to 'be once named (mentioned with approval, or spoken of lightly) among you' — so far should believers be from them that they should not even be a topic of casual or approving talk, 'as becometh (is fitting for) saints' (Barnes, Robertson, Henry).
Calvin: such purity becometh the saints, the holy ones set apart to God; their character demands the utter avoidance of these sins, which are wholly unfit for those consecrated to him.

themes: fornication, and all uncleanness, or covetousness, let it not be once named among you
as becometh saints
the call to utter purity
sin not even named among saints

schools.summary: Paul forbids three sins of speech: 'filthiness (obscenity, shameful talk), foolish talking (morologia — silly, empty speech), nor jesting (eutrapelia — coarse wit, ribald joking)' — 'which are not convenient (not fitting, unbecoming)' for believers; 'jesting' meaning not innocent cheerfulness but the clever, coarse wit that turns easily to indecency, the abuse of humor; 'but rather giving of thanks' — gracious, thankful speech replacing corrupt or empty talk (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).
Wesley: in place of filthy or foolish talk, the speech that becomes saints is gracious and thankful; thanksgiving is the right and wholesome use of the tongue God has given.

themes: neither filthiness, nor foolish talking, nor jesting, which are not convenient
but rather giving of thanks
the call to pure speech
corrupt talk replaced by thanksgiving

schools.summary: The solemn certainty ('this ye know'): 'no whoremonger (fornicator), nor unclean person, nor covetous man' — those habitually given to impurity or greed are shut out; 'who is an idolater' — the covetous man is an idolater, for covetousness is idolatry, the greedy man making a god of his wealth and desire, worshipping the creature instead of the Creator (Colossians 3:5); such persons 'have no inheritance in the kingdom of Christ and of God' — the habitual practice of these sins bars from the kingdom (Barnes, Robertson).

Chrysostom (with Calvin): covetousness is named idolatry because it dethrones God in the heart and sets up gain in his place; the habitual practice of these sins excludes from the inheritance — a sobering warning even to professing believers.

themes: no whoremonger, nor unclean person, nor covetous man, who is an idolater, hath any inheritance
the kingdom of Christ and of God
the impure and covetous excluded from the kingdom
covetousness is idolatry

schools.summary: Paul warns: 'let no man deceive you with vain (empty, hollow) words' — against those who would excuse or make light of these sins with plausible but empty arguments; 'for because of these things (the sins of impurity and covetousness) cometh the wrath of God' — God's wrath surely falls on such sins, they are not trivial but bring down judgment; 'upon the children of disobedience' — those characterized by disobedience to God (the same phrase as 2:2), the impenitent who practise these sins (Barnes, Robertson).

themes: Let no man deceive you with vain words
because of these things cometh the wrath of God upon the children of disobedience
the warning against deceivers
the wrath of God on the disobedient

schools.summary: Paul's conclusion ('therefore'): 'be not ye partakers (symmetochoi — sharers, fellow-participants) with them' — believers are not to join in the sins of the children of disobedience, nor share in their conduct; to be partakers with them would be to take part in their sins and so in their judgment, so believers are to keep clear of both the deeds and the doom of the disobedient (Barnes, Robertson, Gill).

themes: Be not ye therefore partakers with them
the call not to share in the sins of the disobedient
keeping clear of their deeds and doom
separation from sin

schools.summary: The strong figure: not merely in darkness and in light, but believers 'were (themselves) darkness' (so given over to ignorance and sin) and 'now are ye light in the Lord' — themselves become light, illumined and transformed in union with the Lord; the change is 'in the Lord,' believers being light not of themselves but by union with Christ, the light of the world; 'walk as children of light' — live consistently with the new nature, in holiness and truth (Barnes, Robertson).
Wesley (with Calvin): being now light, believers are to walk as children of light, their conduct matching their illumined nature; the new being requires a new walk.

themes: ye were sometimes darkness, but now are ye light in the Lord
walk as children of light
former darkness and present light
the walk of light

schools.summary: The parenthetical explanation of what the walk of light produces: 'the fruit of the Spirit (or, of the light, as the better manuscripts read) is in all goodness and righteousness and truth' — the threefold fruit that the light yields: 'goodness (benevolence, kindness toward others), righteousness (justice, uprightness), and truth (sincerity, integrity, honesty)' — the moral fruit that marks the children of light, standing over against the unfruitful works of darkness (Barnes, Robertson).

themes: the fruit of the Spirit is in all goodness and righteousness and truth
the fruit of the light
goodness, righteousness, and truth
the moral fruit of the children of light

schools.summary: The children of light, in their walk, are 'proving (dokimazo — testing, examining, discerning) what is acceptable (euareston — well-pleasing) unto the Lord' — making it their constant study to find out and do what pleases God; the measure of conduct is not human opinion or custom but 'what is acceptable unto the Lord,' the believer's aim being to please Christ, testing every action by that standard; the walk of light is active and discerning, not passive (Barnes, Robertson, JFB).

themes: Proving what is acceptable unto the Lord
the call to prove what pleases the Lord
testing conduct by God's pleasure
the discerning walk of light

schools.summary: Paul's command: 'have no fellowship (no participation, no sharing) with the unfruitful (barren, profitless) works of darkness' — the deeds of sin, which produce nothing good, are to be wholly avoided; the works of darkness are 'unfruitful,' yielding no good fruit (only shame and ruin), in contrast to the fruit of light (v.9); 'but rather reprove (elencho — expose, convict, rebuke) them' — believers are not merely to abstain but to expose and rebuke the works of darkness by their light and witness (Barnes, Robertson).
Cyril (with Calvin): the children of light must not only avoid the deeds of darkness but expose and convict them; their holy walk and witness are themselves a reproof to sin.

themes: have no fellowship with the unfruitful works of darkness, but rather reprove them
shunning and reproving the works of darkness
the barren deeds of sin
exposing darkness by the light

schools.summary: Paul gives a reason for shunning these works: 'it is a shame (aischron — shameful, disgraceful) even to speak of those things which are done of them (the children of disobedience) in secret' — the deeds of darkness are so vile that merely to describe them is shameful; they are 'done in secret,' hidden, practised in darkness and concealment, for they cannot bear the light; their secrecy is itself a mark of their shamefulness, and explains why believers reprove rather than partake (Barnes, Robertson).

themes: it is a shame even to speak of those things which are done of them in secret
the shameful secret deeds of darkness
sins too vile to describe
done in secret, shunning the light

schools.summary: The principle: 'all things that are reproved (exposed, convicted) are made manifest (brought to light, shown for what they are) by the light' — when the light shines on the works of darkness, it reveals their true nature; the maxim 'whatsoever doth make manifest (reveal, bring to light) is light' — it is the very nature and property of light to reveal, so that which exposes hidden things is itself light; the believer, as light, by his holy life exposes the works of darkness (Barnes, Robertson).
Wesley: the light not only exposes but may transform — what is made manifest by the light may itself become light; the exposing of sin can be the first step toward its conversion.

themes: all things that are reproved are made manifest by the light
whatsoever doth make manifest is light
the light exposing and making manifest
the believer as light unmasking darkness

schools.summary: Ellicott and Robertson (with Clarke): the words are most likely a fragment of an early Christian hymn — the rhythmical character of the Greek, and the impossibility of finding a close scriptural original, point to a sung verse (like the embryo creeds of 1 Corinthians 15:3-4; 1 Timothy 3:16); some early hymn began 'Awake, thou that sleepest...' The summons is to the sinner asleep and dead in sin — 'awake (rouse from spiritual slumber), arise from the dead' — with the promise 'Christ shall give thee light (epiphausei — shall dawn upon thee),' Christ the day-star shining on those who rouse and turn to him.

Calvin (with Barnes, Wesley): Christ is speaking through his ministers in the ordinary message of gospel preaching, freely alluding to the prophecies of Christ's kingdom (Isaiah 60:1; 26:19) rather than citing one verbatim; the gospel constantly cries to the dead to rise to the light of Christ. Barnes doubts any formal quotation is intended, taking Paul to speak as an inspired man; some Fathers (Epiphanius, Syncellus) supposed a lost apocryphal source (Elijah or Jeremiah), and Grotius read 'it (the light) saith.'

debates.question: What is the source of 'Awake thou that sleepest...'?

sides.position: A fragment of an early Christian hymn
Christ speaking through the gospel, freely alluding to Isaiah (60:1; 26:19) — not a verbatim citation
A lost apocryphal prophecy (of Elijah or Jeremiah)

themes: Awake thou that sleepest, and arise from the dead, and Christ shall give thee light
the summons to awake and arise to Christ's light
the crux of the quotation's source
the call to spiritual life

schools.summary: Paul's charge: 'see (take heed) then that ye walk circumspectly (akribos — carefully, accurately, exactly)' — believers are to order their lives with exact care and watchfulness, attentive to their conduct; 'not as fools (the unwise, who live carelessly and without thought) but as wise (the prudent, who order life by true wisdom)' — the choice between a foolish and a wise walk (Barnes, Robertson, Chrysostom).
Henry: the Christian life requires watchful, careful, wise conduct — not the heedless living of the foolish, but the deliberate prudence of the wise, taking heed to every step.

themes: See then that ye walk circumspectly, not as fools, but as wise
the call to walk circumspectly and wisely
exact care in conduct
the choice between foolish and wise walking

schools.summary: The charge: 'redeeming the time (exagorazomenoi ton kairon — buying up the opportunity, making the most of every season)' — believers are to seize and use well every opportunity for good, as a merchant buys up a valuable commodity; time and opportunity are precious and fleeting, and to 'redeem' them is to rescue them from waste and turn them to good account, no opportunity for godliness or service to be lost (Barnes, Robertson).
Wesley (with Calvin): 'the days are evil' — full of sin, danger, and temptation (perhaps also of persecution); the evil of the times makes the wise use of every opportunity the more urgent.

themes: Redeeming the time, because the days are evil
the call to redeem the time
buying up every opportunity for good
the urgency of evil days

schools.summary: Paul's repeated charge: 'be ye not unwise (aphrones — foolish, senseless) but understanding (synientes — perceiving, grasping) what the will of the Lord is' — believers are to apply themselves to know and comprehend God's will; true wisdom consists in understanding what the Lord requires and approves, the wise walk grounded in knowing God's will, not in human cleverness; this sums up the call to wisdom of v.15 (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: be ye not unwise, but understanding what the will of the Lord is
the call to understand the will of the Lord
true wisdom in knowing God's will
the wise walk grounded in God's will

schools.summary: Paul forbids drunkenness: 'be not drunk with wine, wherein is excess (asotia — riot, profligacy, dissoluteness)' — intoxication, which leads to debauchery and the loss of self-control; the striking antithesis 'but be filled with (in, by) the Spirit' — over against the false stimulation and excess of drink, believers are to be filled with the Holy Spirit, who produces a true and holy exhilaration; the filling a continual, present command (Barnes, Robertson).

Cyril (with Wesley): believers are to be ever filled with the Spirit, the source of spiritual joy, song, and thanksgiving (vv.19-20); the Spirit's fulness is the holy counterpart to the empty excess of drink.

themes: be not drunk with wine, wherein is excess; but be filled with the Spirit
the contrast between drunkenness and being filled with the Spirit
the Spirit's holy exhilaration
the continual filling of the Spirit

schools.summary: The fulness of the Spirit overflows in praise: 'speaking to yourselves (to one another) in psalms and hymns and spiritual songs' — believers edifying and encouraging each other through sacred song; the three kinds being 'psalms' (the Old Testament psalms, or songs to accompaniment), 'hymns' (songs of praise to God), and 'spiritual songs' (sacred songs of various kinds, prompted by the Spirit) — a rich variety of praise (Barnes, Robertson).

Cyril (with Wesley): the singing is 'in your heart' (from the heart, with inward devotion, not mere lip-service) and 'to the Lord' (directed to God, for his glory); true praise is the heart's melody offered to God.

themes: Speaking to yourselves in psalms and hymns and spiritual songs
singing and making melody in your heart to the Lord
mutual praise in sacred song
the heart's melody to the Lord

schools.summary: The further mark of the Spirit-filled life: 'giving thanks always (at all times) for all things (in every circumstance) unto God and the Father' — a continual, comprehensive gratitude; thanksgiving is to be 'always' (without ceasing) and 'for all things,' not only for blessings but in every circumstance, even adversity, trusting God's good purpose, the believer finding cause for thanks in all (Barnes, Robertson).

Cyril (with Wesley): thanksgiving is offered 'in the name of (through, by the mediation of) our Lord Jesus Christ' — all our praise and thanks reach God through Christ, the one mediator through whom alone our worship is accepted.

themes: Giving thanks always for all things unto God and the Father
in the name of our Lord Jesus Christ
constant thanksgiving for all things
thanks through Christ the mediator

schools.summary: The transition to the household duties: 'submitting yourselves (hypotassomenoi — yielding, subordinating yourselves) one to another' — a general principle of mutual deference and humility among believers, each yielding to others in love; this is the broad principle that governs the specific relationships now to follow (wives and husbands, children and parents, servants and masters), each fulfilling his proper duty in a spirit of humble submission, though the particular submission differs by relation (Barnes, Robertson, Henry).

Calvin: this submission is 'in the fear of God (or of Christ)' — not from mere social custom but from reverence for God, who appoints these relations and to whom all are accountable; the fear of God is the motive and bond of all right subjection.

themes: Submitting yourselves one to another in the fear of God
the call to mutual submission
humble deference grounding the household duties
submission in the fear of God

schools.summary: The first household duty: 'wives, submit yourselves (the verb carried over from v.21) unto your own husbands' — the wife is to yield to and honour her own husband's leadership in the home; 'as unto the Lord' — the submission rendered as part of her devotion to Christ, as though to the Lord himself, a form of obedience to Christ who appointed the order (Barnes, Robertson, Chrysostom).

Calvin (with Henry): this is no degrading servitude but the honourable order God established; the wife submits not to a tyrant but to a husband bound to love her as Christ loves the church (v.25), and her submission is part of her reverence for the Lord.

themes: Wives, submit yourselves unto your own husbands, as unto the Lord
the call for wives to submit to their husbands
submission as to the Lord
the honourable order God appointed

schools.summary: The ground of the wife's submission: 'the husband is the head of the wife, even as (after the pattern of) Christ is the head of the church' — the husband's headship in the home mirrors Christ's headship over the church; 'head' denoting leadership, authority, and care — not tyranny but the responsible direction a head gives to the body; 'and he is the saviour of the body' — of Christ uniquely added, his being the saviour of the church (Barnes, Robertson).

Cyril (with Calvin): 'saviour of the body' is a glory belonging to Christ alone; yet the husband too is, in his measure, the protector and provider for his wife, leading and caring for her as Christ does the church.

themes: the husband is the head of the wife, even as Christ is the head of the church
he is the saviour of the body
the husband's headship patterned on Christ
leadership and care, not tyranny

schools.summary: The conclusion ('therefore'): 'as the church is subject unto Christ, so let the wives be to their own husbands' — the church's glad subjection to Christ is the model for the wife's submission to her husband; 'in every thing' — in all things lawful and proper, within the sphere of the marriage relation; the submission is comprehensive, though always (as understood) limited by obedience to God, never requiring sin (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).
Wesley: as the church's subjection to Christ is loving and willing, so the wife's submission is to be — not slavish but the glad yielding of love within God's appointed order.

themes: as the church is subject unto Christ, so let the wives be to their own husbands in every thing
the wife's submission patterned on the church
subjection in every thing
willing submission within God's order

schools.summary: The husband's corresponding duty, weightier than the wife's submission: 'husbands, love your wives' — the husband's whole obligation is summed in self-giving love, not mere authority; 'even as (after the pattern of) Christ also loved the church' — the husband's love patterned on Christ's love for the church, the highest conceivable standard; 'and gave himself for it' — Christ's love went to the length of dying for her, the husband's love to be self-sacrificing, ready to give itself wholly for the wife's good (Barnes, Robertson).
Cyril (with Calvin): Christ gave himself for the church in love; the husband's love is to imitate this self-sacrifice, seeking the wife's good even at his own cost, as Christ sought the church's.

themes: Husbands, love your wives, even as Christ also loved the church, and gave himself for it
the call for husbands to love their wives as Christ loved the church
self-sacrificing love
Christ's love the pattern

schools.summary: The purpose of Christ's self-giving: 'that he might sanctify (set apart, make holy) and cleanse (purify) it (the church)' — Christ gave himself not only to redeem but to purify the church, making her holy; 'with the washing of water by the word' — most take this as referring to baptism (the outward washing) joined with the word (the gospel, which gives baptism its meaning and power), the cleansing being by the word applied, of which baptism is the sign (Barnes, Robertson).

Wesley (with Calvin): the outward washing signifies the inward cleansing of the soul by the word and Spirit; the word is the instrument by which Christ purifies his church, the sign and the thing signified going together.

themes: that he might sanctify and cleanse it with the washing of water by the word
Christ's purpose to sanctify and cleanse the church
the washing of water by the word
baptism and the word

schools.summary: The ultimate purpose of Christ's sanctifying work: 'that he might present it (the church) to himself a glorious church (endoxon — in splendour, radiant)' — Christ will at last present the church to himself in perfect glory; the figure of a flawless bride, 'not having spot (stain), or wrinkle (mark of age or decay), or any such thing' — the church perfectly pure and beautiful, free from every defect; 'but that it should be holy and without blemish (amomos — unblemished, faultless)' (Barnes, Robertson).

Cyril (with Calvin): the church will be holy and without blemish — the final, perfect holiness of the bride made spotless for her husband Christ; this is the goal and consummation of all his sanctifying work.

themes: that he might present it to himself a glorious church, not having spot, or wrinkle
that it should be holy and without blemish
Christ presenting the church glorious
the spotless bride

schools.summary: The inference ('so'): from Christ's love for the church, 'men ought to love their wives as their own bodies' — with the same care a man naturally has for his own body, since husband and wife are one flesh; 'he that loveth his wife loveth himself' — because the two are one, the husband who loves his wife is, in effect, loving himself, her good being his own (Barnes, Robertson).
Wesley (with Calvin): to love one's wife is not a burden but the most natural thing, for she is part of oneself; the husband cares for her as for his own body, her welfare bound up with his.

themes: So ought men to love their wives as their own bodies
He that loveth his wife loveth himself
loving the wife as one's own body
husband and wife one flesh

schools.summary: The appeal to nature: 'no man ever yet hated his own flesh (his own body), but nourisheth (ektrephei — feeds, sustains) and cherisheth (thalpei — warms, fosters, tends, as a bird warms its young) it' — every man naturally cares for and tends his own body; the husband is to care for his wife with such nourishing tenderness, 'even as the Lord (Christ) the church' (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin (with Wesley): Christ nourishes and cherishes the church with tender, sustaining care, and the husband's care for his wife is to mirror it; the love is not cold authority but warm, fostering tenderness.

themes: no man ever yet hated his own flesh; but nourisheth and cherisheth it
even as the Lord the church
the husband nourishing his wife as Christ the church
tender, fostering care

schools.summary: The ground of Christ's care for the church: 'we are members of his body' — believers are united to Christ as members to a body, parts of him, sharing his life, which is why he nourishes and cherishes the church; 'of his flesh, and of his bones' — the strong figure (echoing Genesis 2:23, Adam's words of Eve) expressing the closest possible union between Christ and believers, as intimate as that of Adam and Eve, or of body and members (Barnes, Robertson).
Wesley (with Calvin): the believer's union with Christ is so close that we are reckoned part of him, his flesh and bones; such is the intimacy of the mystical union between Christ and his church.

themes: we are members of his body, of his flesh, and of his bones
believers as members of Christ's body
the closest union with Christ
the mystical union echoing Genesis 2:23

schools.summary: Paul quotes Genesis 2:24 (the institution of marriage): 'for this cause shall a man leave his father and mother, and shall be joined (proskollethesetai — cleave, be glued) unto his wife, and they two shall be one flesh' — the foundational statement of the marriage union; 'one flesh' expressing the intimate, indissoluble union of husband and wife (no longer two but one, the closest of human bonds, taking precedence even over the tie to parents); cited because it pictures the union of Christ and the church (v.32, Barnes, Robertson).

themes: a man leave his father and mother, and shall be joined unto his wife, and they two shall be one flesh
the one flesh of marriage cited from Genesis
the indissoluble marriage union
marriage picturing Christ and the church

schools.summary: 'Mystery (mysterion)' means something once concealed, hidden, before unknown, now revealed (as in 1:9) — here the profound truth that the eternal Son of God should form so intimate a union with his people, of which marriage is a figure; 'great' because so deep. Paul's express words 'but I speak concerning Christ and the church' show (Barnes strongly: solely; Robertson, Ellicott: chiefly) that his statement of the mystery refers to the union of Christ and the church, the antitype, not to marriage as such. They reject the Roman Catholic inference that marriage is a sacrament, which arose only from the Vulgate's rendering of mysterion by 'sacramentum' — a verbal accident, not the sense of the Greek (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott, Clarke).

Calvin: he is overwhelmed by the depth of this spiritual union between Christ and the church, which no language can explain — let us labour rather to feel Christ living in us than to discover its nature. He sharply rejects the Papists' use of this verse to make marriage a sacrament: they were deceived by the Latin 'sacramentum' translating the Greek mysterion (the same word used in this Epistle of the calling of the Gentiles); the blunder 'arose from the grossest ignorance,' for marriage is no sacred symbol of grace like Baptism or the Supper, and Paul's own correction ('I speak of Christ and the church') guards against the mistake.

debates.question: Does 'this is a great mystery' prove that marriage is a sacrament?

sides.position: No — 'mystery' is a profound revealed truth (the union of Christ and the church, figured by marriage); the sacrament-reading rests only on the Vulgate's 'sacramentum' for mysterion
Yes — marriage is a sacrament (Roman Catholic, from the Vulgate 'sacramentum hoc magnum est')
holders: the Church of Rome (rejected by the Protestant voices)

themes: This is a great mystery: but I speak concerning Christ and the church
the great mystery of Christ and the church
the crux of marriage as a sacrament
the Vulgate's sacramentum rejected

schools.summary: Paul's practical summary ('nevertheless,' returning from the digression on Christ and the church to the immediate duty): 'let every one of you in particular so love his wife even as himself' — each husband to love his own wife as he loves himself; 'and the wife see that she reverence (phobetai — fear, in the sense of respect, honour) her husband' — the wife to give her husband reverence and honour; the love and the reverence answering to each other (Barnes, Robertson).

Cyril (with Calvin): the two duties together — the husband's self-giving love and the wife's reverent honour — make the Christian marriage a reflection of Christ and the church, each fulfilling the proper part of the one bond.

themes: let every one of you in particular so love his wife even as himself
the wife see that she reverence her husband
the summary duty of love and reverence
marriage reflecting Christ and the church
